brand.name: StackScout
brand.tagline: The public scout deck for serious builder tools.
brand.description: StackScout tracks the tools, services, APIs, MCPs, CLIs, and workbench apps worth a builder's attention.
brand.heroEyebrow: Curated ecosystem // tools, systems, and builder intelligence
brand.heroTitle: A cleaner way to track the tools actually shaping modern workflows.
brand.heroLede: StackScout is the public-facing scout deck for fast-moving builder infrastructure: websites, web services, APIs, MCPs, CLIs, and a smaller lab lane for the tools we are building ourselves. It is curated, selective, and designed to stay useful rather than exhaustive.

heroFacts: Curated ecosystem first, not an everything directory
Badges and freshness over fake precision scoring
Official links, update trails, and use-case context in one place

categories.title: Websites
categories.description: Browser-first products and hubs that are useful on their own, not just as a wrapper around an API.

categories.title: Web Services
categories.description: Hosted workflow and automation platforms that carry real operational value without requiring a local runtime first.

categories.title: APIs
categories.description: Platform surfaces for shipping structured AI, automation, and developer workflows.

categories.title: MCPs
categories.description: Model Context Protocol servers and bridges that turn static assistants into tool-using systems.

categories.title: CLIs
categories.description: Command-line tools that earn a place in a serious builder workflow.

categories.title: Web Apps
categories.description: Focused public-facing applications that sit closer to products than infrastructure.

categories.title: Desktop Apps
categories.description: Private or local-first desktop surfaces that are notable enough to track publicly.

badges.label: Recommended
badges.description: Worth active attention now. Strong fit, solid signal, and easy to justify to other builders.

badges.label: Specialist Pick
badges.description: Excellent in a narrower lane, even if it is not the first tool every builder should start with.

badges.label: Worth Watching
badges.description: Not a full recommendation yet, but moving in an interesting enough direction to track.

badges.label: Early but Promising
badges.description: Still forming. Included because the shape is compelling, not because it is already complete.

maturity.label: Stable
maturity.description: Operationally dependable enough to recommend without caveat-heavy framing.

maturity.label: Fast-moving
maturity.description: Shipping quickly, improving visibly, and still changing shape month to month.

maturity.label: Experimental
maturity.description: Interesting, usable in parts, but still clearly in discovery.

maturity.label: Legacy
maturity.description: Still worth knowing about, but no longer where the momentum is.

methodology.thesis: StackScout is a public guide, not a benchmark lab. We badge selectively, update from official sources where possible, and avoid pretending every fast-moving tool deserves a fixed numeric score.

principles.title: Curated over exhaustive
principles.body: We start with a smaller cohort of tools that matter, then widen coverage once the update pipeline is trustworthy.

principles.title: Official sources first
principles.body: Release notes, changelogs, product blogs, docs, and official repos outrank secondary coverage.

principles.title: Badges over fake precision
principles.body: Public verdicts use editorial badges and short written theses instead of fragile decimal scoring.

principles.title: Freshness must be visible
principles.body: Every record carries dates. If something has not been updated recently, the site should say that plainly.

freshnessPolicy: Tool records carry the date of the latest tracked change that informed the public entry.
Update feed entries carry the source date and source link.
A stale entry is better than a fake live one; honesty beats noise.
reviewLimits: No private operational details in the public layer.
No claims of integration depth we have not verified or sourced.

radar.title: Azure MCP Server
radar.summary: Microsoft's Azure MCP surface is still in public preview, but the official server is broad enough to matter for enterprise agent workflows.
radar.reason: Broad Azure coverage, official backing, and a credible enterprise story.

radar.title: Hugging Face MCP Server
radar.summary: Hugging Face now has an official MCP story for the Hub, which could become one of the easier ways to bring model, dataset, and Space context into agentic workflows.
radar.reason: Strong overlap with model discovery, repo knowledge, and public AI artifacts.

radar.title: Make MCP Toolboxes
radar.summary: Make is pushing MCP deeper into its automation story via dedicated toolboxes that expose curated scenario sets to assistants.
radar.reason: Interesting bridge between low-code automation and agent-native tool surfaces.

radar.title: GitHub skills ecosystem
radar.summary: GitHub CLI's new skills commandset hints at a broader portable-agent workflow ecosystem worth tracking alongside MCP.
radar.reason: Portable instructions and agent tooling are becoming a real product layer.

collections.title: Best tools for creators
collections.summary: A practical mix for publishing, generation, research, and creative flow.
collections.description: If you are building content systems, prototyping creative workflows, or stitching research into production, this is the first StackScout lane to start with.

collections.title: Best MCPs to try
collections.summary: The MCP servers that are easiest to justify in a real builder workflow right now.
collections.description: A good starter set should teach you what MCP is actually useful for: repo intelligence, browser automation, and system-specific reach.

collections.title: Best APIs for automation
collections.summary: The model and platform APIs that matter when your workflows need structured output, tool use, and long-horizon reasoning.
collections.description: This collection is biased toward agents, orchestration, and production work rather than generic chatbot embedding.

collections.title: Best offline-friendly tools
collections.summary: Local or local-first tools that stay useful even when the browser tabs are closed.
collections.description: The best offline-friendly tools keep control close to the operator while still fitting a modern workflow.

collections.title: From the StackScout Lab
collections.summary: A clearly labelled subset of the tools we are building ourselves.
collections.description: These are not presented as the whole point of the site. They sit alongside the wider ecosystem and earn their place separately.
